posts.caption: The 2-minute rule that actually killed my procrastination
posts.transcript:
Everyone tells you to just start, but that advice is useless without a trigger. Here's the version that worked for me: if a task takes less than two minutes, you are not allowed to write it down. You do it now.

The magic isn't the two minutes. It's that you stop maintaining a list of tiny obligations that drain you all day. The list itself was the weight, not the tasks.

For anything bigger, shrink the first step until it fits inside two minutes. Don't write the report. Open the document and type one ugly sentence. Starting is the only part your brain resists, so make starting trivially small.
posts.content: The 2-minute rule that actually killed my procrastination. Everyone tells you to just start...
posts.creator: jamesclear

posts.caption: Why your scrambled eggs are rubbery (it's the heat, not the eggs)
posts.transcript:
Rubbery eggs come from one mistake: high heat. Hot pans cook the proteins so fast they squeeze out all the water, and that's the rubber you taste.

Low and slow. Cold nonstick pan, eggs in, then turn the heat to medium-low. Stir constantly with a spatula, pulling the curds from the edges into the center. It should take three to four minutes, not thirty seconds.

Pull them off the heat while they still look slightly wet. Carryover heat finishes them on the plate. Salt at the end, never at the start, or you'll draw the water out early.
posts.content: Why your scrambled eggs are rubbery. Rubbery eggs come from one mistake: high heat...
posts.creator: bonappetit

posts.caption: Index funds explained in 60 seconds
posts.transcript:
An index fund is a basket that holds a little bit of every company in a market, like the S&P 500. Instead of betting on one stock, you own a slice of all of them.

Why it works: most active managers, the people paid to pick winners, fail to beat the index over time. After fees, the simple basket wins. You're not trying to be clever. You're trying to capture the average, which is surprisingly hard to beat.

The cost matters more than anything. A fund charging 0.03 percent versus one charging 1 percent doesn't sound like much, but over thirty years it can eat a third of your returns. Pick the cheap basket and stop touching it.
posts.content: Index funds explained in 60 seconds. An index fund is a basket...
posts.creator: theplainbagel

posts.caption: 5 typography rules I wish I knew sooner — swipe for examples
posts.transcript: Most beginner layouts fail for the same five reasons, and they're all about type, not color.
posts.slides:
Rule 1 — Line length. Aim for 45 to 75 characters per line. Too wide and the eye loses its place returning to the next line.

Rule 2 — Line height. Body text wants roughly 1.5x the font size. Headlines want less, around 1.1, because big text already has visual gaps.

Rule 3 — Hierarchy with size, not weight. Two weights is plenty. Make things big or small before you make them bold.

Rule 4 — Limit your fonts. One serif for reading, one sans for interface. A third font almost never earns its place.

Rule 5 — Align to a grid. Pick a spacing unit, usually 8 pixels, and make every margin a multiple of it. Consistency reads as quality.
posts.content: 5 typography rules. Line length, line height, hierarchy, limit fonts, align to a grid.
posts.creator: designjoy

posts.caption: Morning sunlight and your sleep — the 10 minute habit
posts.transcript:
If you fix one thing about your sleep, make it morning light. Within the first hour of waking, get outside for ten minutes without sunglasses.

Light hitting your eyes early sets a timer. Roughly sixteen hours later, melatonin release begins, and you get sleepy on schedule. Skip the morning light and that timer drifts later every day.

On a cloudy day you need longer, maybe twenty minutes, because outdoor light even under clouds is far brighter than any indoor bulb. And it has to be real outdoor light. Through a window doesn't count; glass filters out the wavelengths that matter most.
posts.content: Morning sunlight and your sleep. Within the first hour of waking, get outside...
posts.creator: hubermanlab

posts.caption: How to actually read a research paper without crying
posts.transcript:
Stop reading research papers top to bottom. Nobody does it that way, not even the people who write them.

Read the abstract, then jump straight to the figures. The figures are the actual findings; the rest is justification. If you understand the charts, you understand the paper.

Only then read the discussion section, where the authors tell you what they think it means and, more usefully, what the limitations are. Skip the methods unless you plan to replicate it. You can always come back. Reading order is a tool, not a rule.
posts.content: How to read a research paper. Read the abstract, then jump to the figures...
posts.creator: andrewhuberman

posts.caption: The 50/30/20 budget, but make it realistic
posts.transcript:
The 50/30/20 rule says half your income goes to needs, thirty percent to wants, twenty percent to savings. It's a fine starting point and a terrible finishing point.

The problem is it pretends everyone's rent is the same. If you live in an expensive city, needs eat sixty or seventy percent, and pretending otherwise just makes you feel like a failure every month.

Use the ratios as a mirror, not a cage. Track where your money actually goes for one month, no judgment. Then move one category by five percent. Small, boring, repeatable. That's the whole game.
posts.content: The 50/30/20 budget, but make it realistic. Use the ratios as a mirror, not a cage.
posts.creator: thefinancialdiet

posts.caption: Prompting tip: ask the model to think before it answers
posts.transcript:
The single highest-leverage prompting trick is also the dullest: tell the model to reason step by step before giving its answer.

Models generate one token at a time, so if you force the conclusion first, it has to commit before it's done thinking. Ask for the reasoning first and the final answer last, and accuracy on anything involving logic or math jumps noticeably.

The second trick is to give it an out. Add the line: if you're not sure, say so. Without permission to be uncertain, a model will confidently invent an answer every single time. You're not making it smarter, you're removing the pressure to bluff.
posts.content: Prompting tip: ask the model to think before it answers. Reason step by step...
posts.creator: designjoy

posts.caption: One stretch for desk-tight hips, every morning
posts.transcript:
Sitting all day shortens your hip flexors, and tight hips quietly wreck your lower back. One stretch fixes most of it: the kneeling hip flexor stretch.

Kneel on one knee, other foot flat in front, knee over ankle. Now tuck your pelvis under, like you're pointing your belt buckle up. That tuck is the whole stretch; most people skip it and just lean forward, which does nothing.

Hold thirty seconds each side, breathing slowly. Do it once in the morning and once after a long sitting block. You're not trying to get flexible in a day, you're undoing eight hours of a chair.
posts.content: One stretch for desk-tight hips. The kneeling hip flexor stretch...
posts.creator: hubermanlab
